---
title: "Data Pipeline Overview"
---

The data pipeline captures robot sensor streams on edge devices, compresses and chunks the recordings for efficient transfer, uploads them to Azure Blob Storage, and converts the raw ROS 2 bag files into training-ready datasets compatible with LeRobot. Every stage is designed for the constraints of edge hardware — limited disk, intermittent connectivity, and real-time sensor rates that can exceed 200 Hz.

![A robotic sensor suite capturing multi-modal data streams during a manipulation task. High-fidelity recording is the foundation of imitation learning pipelines.
*Image: [KUKA robot for teaching](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:KUKA_robot_for_teaching.jpg) by Mixabest, [CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)*
](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/KUKA_robot_for_teaching.jpg/640px-KUKA_robot_for_teaching.jpg){fig-alt="KUKA industrial robot arm in a teaching environment" width="70%"}

## Pipeline Architecture

The data pipeline spans three domains: edge capture, cloud ingestion, and dataset assembly. Each domain operates asynchronously — recordings accumulate on-device, upload when bandwidth is available, and conversion runs as an event-driven cloud pipeline.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-data-pipeline
%%| fig-cap: "End-to-end data pipeline from edge sensors through cloud storage to training-ready datasets."
flowchart LR
    subgraph Edge["Edge Device (Jetson)"]
        ROS["ROS 2 Topics\n(cameras, joints, IMU)"] --> REC["Recording Service\n(rosbag2 + MCAP)"]
        REC --> CHUNK["Chunked Bag Files\n(1 GB / 5 min splits)"]
        CHUNK --> COMP["Compression\n(zstd / lz4)"]
        COMP --> VAL["Local Validation\n(gap detection)"]
    end

    subgraph Upload["Transfer"]
        VAL --> AZ["Azure Blob Upload\n(multipart, incremental)"]
    end

    subgraph Cloud["Azure Cloud"]
        AZ --> BLOB["Blob Storage\n(raw bags)"]
        BLOB --> EVT["Event Grid\nTrigger"]
        EVT --> CONV["ROS → LeRobot\nConversion"]
        CONV --> QA["Quality Gates\n(frame counts, gaps)"]
        QA --> DS["Versioned Dataset\n(LeRobot format)"]
    end

    DS --> TRAIN["Training Pipeline"]
```

## Recording Configuration

The recording service uses a Pydantic-validated YAML configuration that specifies which ROS 2 topics to capture, at what frequency, and with which compression algorithm. The configuration model enforces constraints at parse time — frequencies within sensor capabilities, valid compression choices, and GPIO pin ranges for hardware triggers.

```{bash}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
# Reference recording configuration for a UR10e manipulation setup
cat <<'EOF'
# yaml-language-server: $schema=./recording_config.schema.json

topics:
  - name: /joint_states
    frequency_hz: 100.0
    compression: lz4

  - name: /camera/color/image_raw
    frequency_hz: 30.0
    compression: zstd

  - name: /camera/depth/image_raw
    frequency_hz: 30.0
    compression: zstd

  - name: /imu/data
    frequency_hz: 200.0
    compression: lz4

trigger:
  type: gpio
  pin: 17
  active_high: true

disk_thresholds:
  warning_percent: 80
  critical_percent: 95

gap_detection:
  threshold_ms: 100.0
  severity: warning

output_dir: /data/recordings
EOF
```

### Topic Compression Strategy

Compression choice balances CPU overhead against storage savings. On ARM-based Jetson hardware, CPU headroom is limited during active inference, making algorithm selection critical.

| Algorithm | Image Ratio | Sensor Ratio | Encode Speed (ARM) | CPU Cost | Recommended Use |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|----------|-----------------|
| `none` | 1.0× | 1.0× | N/A | 0% | Debugging, short captures |
| `lz4` | 1.5–2.0× | 2.0–3.0× | ~500 MB/s | <10% | High-frequency streams (≥100 Hz) |
| `zstd` | 2.5–6.0× | 3.0–10.0× | 50–300 MB/s | 20–30% | Images, bandwidth-limited uploads |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Compression Rule of Thumb
Use `lz4` for any topic above 50 Hz — the CPU cost is negligible and it prevents disk saturation. Use `zstd` for image topics where the higher compression ratio significantly reduces upload time over constrained network links.
:::

### Episode Triggers

Episodes are the fundamental unit of training data. Four trigger types control when recording starts and stops:

| Trigger | Mechanism | Use Case |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| `gpio` | Physical button or switch (BCM pin numbering) | Teleoperation demonstrations |
| `position` | Robot joints reach a target pose within tolerance | Repeatable task setups |
| `vr` | VR controller button press (left/right, trigger/grip) | Immersive teleoperation |
| `time` | Fixed duration windows | Autonomous data collection |

## MCAP Storage Format

All recordings use the [MCAP](https://mcap.dev) container format instead of the default SQLite3 backend. MCAP provides chunk-level compression, sequential write optimization, and inline chunk manifests that enable partial reads without scanning the entire file.

```{bash}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
# Record with MCAP format, 1 GB bag splits, and 5-minute duration limits
ros2 bag record \
  --storage mcap \
  --max-bag-size 1073741824 \
  --max-bag-duration 300 \
  --max-cache-size 104857600 \
  /joint_states /camera/color/image_raw /camera/depth/image_raw /imu/data
```

### Chunking Parameters

Bag files are split by size and duration to enable incremental uploads and parallel processing:

| Parameter | Default | Recommended | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|-------------|---------|
| `--max-bag-size` | 0 (no split) | 1 GB | Split at size threshold |
| `--max-bag-duration` | 0 (no split) | 300 s (5 min) | Split at duration threshold |
| `--max-cache-size` | 100 MB | 100 MB | In-memory write buffer |
| `--storage` | sqlite3 | `mcap` | Storage backend |

## Storage Capacity Planning

Full sensor suite recording (RGB + depth + joints + IMU) generates substantial data volumes. Capacity planning is essential for edge devices with limited NVMe storage.

| Device | Storage | Raw Duration | With zstd-3 | With zstd-6 |
|--------|---------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Orin Nano (SSD) | 60 GB | 32 min | 51 min | 1 h 17 min |
| Orin Nano + M.2 | 200 GB | 2 h 8 min | 3 h 23 min | 5 h 8 min |
| AGX Orin (NVMe) | 450 GB | 4 h 47 min | 7 h 37 min | 11 h 32 min |

::: {.callout-warning}
## Disk Monitoring
The recording service monitors disk usage continuously. At the `warning_percent` threshold (default 80%), it logs a warning. At `critical_percent` (default 95%), recording stops immediately to prevent filesystem corruption. Plan upload cadence around these limits.
:::

## Cloud Upload and Ingestion

Completed bag chunks upload to Azure Blob Storage via the Azure Arc-connected data agent. Uploads are incremental and resumable — each chunk is a standalone file that can transfer independently. An Azure Event Grid trigger fires on blob creation, initiating the cloud-side conversion pipeline.

```{bash}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
# Upload recorded bags to Azure Blob Storage
az storage blob upload-batch \
  --account-name "${STORAGE_ACCOUNT}" \
  --destination "raw-recordings/${ROBOT_ID}/${SESSION_ID}" \
  --source /data/recordings/ \
  --pattern "*.mcap" \
  --overwrite false
```

## ROS-to-LeRobot Conversion

The conversion pipeline transforms raw ROS 2 bag files into the LeRobot dataset format — a structured directory of HDF5 episode files with synchronized observation and action tensors. The conversion process:

1. **Deserializes** MCAP messages using the embedded schema definitions.
2. **Aligns** multi-rate topics to a common timeline using nearest-neighbor interpolation.
3. **Extracts** image frames as tensors and joint states as action vectors.
4. **Segments** continuous recordings into discrete episodes based on trigger markers.
5. **Validates** frame counts, temporal gaps, and data integrity.
6. **Writes** LeRobot-compatible HDF5 files with metadata.

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
from lerobot.common.datasets.lerobot_dataset import LeRobotDataset

dataset = LeRobotDataset.from_folder(
    root="data/converted/ur10e-pick-place",
    split="train",
)

# Inspect episode structure
print(f"Episodes: {dataset.num_episodes}")
print(f"Frames per episode: {dataset.num_frames}")
print(f"Observation keys: {dataset.meta.observation_keys}")
print(f"Action dimension: {dataset.meta.action_dim}")
```

## Quality Gates

Every converted dataset passes through automated quality validation before it becomes available for training:

| Check | Criterion | Action on Failure |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|
| Frame count | Minimum frames per episode | Quarantine episode |
| Temporal gaps | No gaps exceeding threshold | Flag for review |
| Sensor completeness | All configured topics present | Reject dataset |
| Image integrity | Decodable frames, correct resolution | Quarantine frame |
| Action bounds | Joint commands within safe limits | Reject episode |

::: {.callout-note}
## Dataset Versioning
Converted datasets are registered in the dataset catalog with a content-addressable version hash. Training jobs reference specific dataset versions, ensuring reproducibility. Re-running conversion on the same raw data produces the same version hash — the pipeline is deterministic.
:::

## Platform-Specific Configurations

The recording configuration adapts to different robot platforms. A mobile manipulator adds LiDAR and odometry topics with tighter gap detection thresholds, while a fixed-base arm focuses on high-frequency joint states and camera feeds. The Pydantic configuration model validates platform-specific constraints — for example, ensuring that position trigger tolerances match the robot's joint count.
